coredata.dc:title: Effect of modified graphene quantum dots on photocatalytic degradation property
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Jiexin
Li, Donghui
Wang, Xiaomin
coredata.dc:description:
The effective use of solar energy in sewage disposal has been extensively investigated. This work focuses on the photocatalytic property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polymer-modified GQDs under visible light. A hydrothermal synthesis route to GQDs was developed by using citric acid as a carbon precursor. The GQDs were modified with polyethylenimine (PEI) and polyethylene glycol (PEG). The obtained GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs were characterized and their structural information was determined through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence spectroscopy, and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Results revealed that the GQDs were uniform in size (2–5nm) and rich in oxygen-containing groups. The GQDs exhibited a strong blue and excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior under excitation wavelengths of 320–420nm. The photocatalytic performance of these samples was demonstrated on the basis of methylene blue (MB) degradation. The photocatalytic rates of GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs decreased successively. The polymer-modified GQDs could qualitatively control the degradation rate of MB. Free radical species were generated to oxidize MB under light irradiation. Thus, photocatalytic organic matter degradation, sustained drug release, and tracking can be combined to implement proper sewage disposal.
Prime novelty The main object of this work is to find out a novel property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as efficient nanomaterials for degradation of organic pollutant dyes under visible light irradiation. And, the GQDs exhibited a strong blue and excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior under excitation wavelengths of 320–420nm. Moreover, the degradation rate could be qualitatively controlled by using different polymer-modified GQDs. Thus, photocatalytic organic matter degradation, sustained drug release, and tracking can be combined to implement proper sewage disposal. Also, the degradation mechanism is discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Organic matter degradation
Photocatalytic property
Polymer-modified

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, GQDs-PEIs and GQDs-PEGs
The photocatalytic activities of the samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51572184 , 51372160 , and 51172152 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the GQDs, (b) the corresponding size distribution, and (c) high-resolution TEM image of single GQD.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs -PEGs. UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of GQDs (b), GQDs-PEIs (c), and GQDs-PEGs (d) at different excitation wavelengths of 320–500nm.
(a) Raman spectra of GQDs and (b) FTIR spectra of PEG, PEI, GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs.
Ultraviolet absorption spectrum of MB solution with (a) GQDs, (b) GQDs-PEIs, (c) GQDs-PEGs in different time and (d) photodegradation rate curve.
ESR spectra of DMPO– •OH (a) and DMPO-••O−2 (b) adducts in the system of PZM/DMPO GQDs before and after visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Effect of modified graphene quantum dots on photocatalytic degradation property

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective use of solar energy in sewage disposal has been extensively investigated. This work focuses on the photocatalytic property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polymer-modified GQDs under visible light. A hydrothermal synthesis route to GQDs was developed by using citric acid as a carbon precursor. The GQDs were modified with polyethylenimine (PEI) and polyethylene glycol (PEG). The obtained GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs were characterized and their structural information was determined through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence spectroscopy, and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Results revealed that the GQDs were uniform in size (2–5nm) and rich in oxygen-containing groups. The GQDs exhibited a strong blue and excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior under excitation wavelengths of 320–420nm. The photocatalytic performance of these samples was demonstrated on the basis of methylene blue (MB) degradation. The photocatalytic rates of GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs decreased successively. The polymer-modified GQDs could qualitatively control the degradation rate of MB. Free radical species were generated to oxidize MB under light irradiation. Thus, photocatalytic organic matter degradation, sustained drug release, and tracking can be combined to implement proper sewage disposal.

The main object of this work is to find out a novel property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as efficient nanomaterials for degradation of organic pollutant dyes under visible light irradiation. And, the GQDs exhibited a strong blue and excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior under excitation wavelengths of 320–420nm. Moreover, the degradation rate could be qualitatively controlled by using different polymer-modified GQDs. Thus, photocatalytic organic matter degradation, sustained drug release, and tracking can be combined to implement proper sewage disposal. Also, the degradation mechanism is discussed.

A novel property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as efficient nanomaterials for degradation of organic pollutant dyes (methylene blue, MB) under visible light irradiation is found. Moreover, the degradation rate could be qualitatively controlled by using different polymer-modified GQDs. Also, the degradation mechanism is discussed. Thus, photocatalytic organic matter degradation, sustained drug release, and tracking can be combined to implement proper sewage disposal.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Energy shortage and environmental pollution are major problems worldwide. As a newly developed energy source, solar energy is abundant, renewable, and pollution-free. The effective utilization of solar energy for environmental pollution treatment and clean energy conversion was extensively investigated. Functional materials, such as photocatalytic semiconductors, have been highly considered for solar energy applications [1]. These materials can directly utilize solar energy to degrade organic pollutants [2]; however, most of these materials are applicable in the UV region only. Zhou et al. [3] used complex photocatalysts, namely, rutile TiO2/GQDs and anatase TiO2/GQDs systems, to harness the visible spectrum of sunlight and thus allow photocatalytic organic matter degradation in visible light. However, insufficient research was performed to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with an excitation-independent behavior for the visible light photocatalytic degradation of organic matter [4]. GQDs [5–8] are new types of quantum dots that were widely explored because of their pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects. GQDs display numerous novel physical and chemical properties [9–12], such as stable photoluminescence, thermal conductivity, and enhanced surface grafting; as such, GQDs are considered as promising materials of optoelectronic devices [13,14]. Pan et al. [9] prepared GQDs with bright blue photoluminescence by cutting oxidized graphene sheets through a hydrothermal method. In addition, a simple, reliable, and reproducible approach was developed to directly prepare functional GQDs with uniform size (3–5nm) and green luminescence [15]. The optical properties and surface modification of GQDs were also examined [14,16].

High concentrations of organic pollutants and microorganisms have been detected in wastewater. Microorganisms are treated using drugs with polymers as a carrier. Polymer-modified GQDs [17] likely combine photocatalytic organic matter degradation, sustained drug release, and tracking. Polyethylenimine (PEI) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) were applied in biopharmaceutical research [18,19] and were used to modify GQDs. This study examined the visible light catalytic degradation property of GQDs and its effects on the photocatalytic degradation property of polymer-modified GQDs. The prepared GQDs exhibit uniform size distribution and excitation-independent photoluminescence [20]. This study also investigated the structural and optical properties of the GQDs in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, GQDs-PEIs and GQDs-PEGs
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (2.52g; TianJin ZhiYuan Reagent Co., Ltd.) and urea (2.16g; TianJin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd.) were dissolved in 60mL of deionized water and stirred to form a clear solution. The solution was then transferred to a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 160°C in an electric oven and maintained at the same temperature for another 4h. The final product was collected by adding ethanol to the solution. The resulting solution was then centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min. Afterward, the solution was concentrated in a lyophilizer (Beijing Boyikang Experimental Instrument Co., Ltd.). PEI and PEG (Mw=10,000; Aladdin Reagents [Shanghai] Co., Ltd.) were employed as modifying compounds because of their environment-friendly properties [21].PEI (0.2g) was added to an aqueous solution of GQDs (50mL, 1mg/mL) with thorough stirring. The solution was transferred to a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated to 200°C, and maintained for 20h. The colloidal solution was cooled to room temperature, purified, and separated through column chromatography on a silica gel to remove the excess PEI. For comparison, GQDs-PEGs were prepared with the same preparation process used for GQDs-PEIs. However, the solution was autoclaved for 24h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic activities of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activities of the samples were evaluated on the basis of methylene blue (MB; Tianjin HengXing Reagent Co., Ltd.) degradation at room temperature. GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs (1mL, 2mg/mL) were added to MB aqueous solution (50mL, 10mg/L). The suspension was protected from light and magnetically stirred for 1h to reach adsorption equilibrium. A 300W Xe lamp was used as a light source with a 400nm cutoff filter to provide visible light irradiation. The distance between the light and the solution was approximately 15cm. At given intervals, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the three samples were obtained. MB was determined spectrophotometrically at λmax =664nm. The photocatalytic degradation rate of MB was calculated by using the following equation: E=(A0−A)/A0×100%, where A0 is the adsorption equilibrium absorbance of MB and A is the absorbance of the MB solution at irradiation time.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples dispersed in deionized water were obtained with a FluroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Japan). Absorption measurements were performed using a Hitachi U-3900 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Morphological analysis was conducted with a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2010). The FT-IR spectrum of the samples was obtained with a Bruker Tensor 27 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

The TEM image (Fig. 1a) shows that the collected GQDs are monodispersed with a mean diameter of 3.34nm. The discernible lattice structures of the GQDs in the TEM images indicate that the resultant nanoparticles exhibit a graphitic nature. A representative HRTEM image (Fig. 1c) displays a lattice spacing of approximately 2.5Å, which is very near to that of graphene (1120) facets [22]. These results suggest that the nanoparticles may be composed of nanocrystalline cores of graphene sp 2 carbon atoms.

The solutions of GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs emit blue luminescence when they are excited with a 365nm UV beam. Fig. 2a shows the UV–Vis spectrum of the as-prepared GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs. The UV–Vis spectrum reveals a clear absorption band at 218nm that can be attributed to the π-π* electron transition of CC. This result is similar to that of GQDs prepared by a hydrothermal graphene oxide reduction method [23]. The GQDs also showed a clear absorption peak at 354nm that corresponds to n-π* transition of the CO bond. The GQDs-PEIs and GQDs-PEGs showed absorption peaks at 328 and 332nm, respectively. The CO bond absorption peak exhibited a position deviation, which likely corresponded to different substituents around the CO bond.

Fig. 2 also illustrates the PL spectra of GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs, which were measured at different excitation wavelengths with the concentrations being optimized. The excitation wavelength dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity is a common phenomenon observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials. These behaviors may reflect not only the effects of differently sized particles in a sample but also the distribution of different emissive sites on each nanoparticle [24]. Fig. 2b shows that the emission wavelength of GQDs exhibited an almost excitation-independent photoluminescence at 320–420nm excitation wavelength, with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 420 and 450nm, respectively. The excitation-independent emission of the GQDs suggests that the size and surface state of the sp
2 clusters contained in GQDs are possibly uniform. GQDs-PEIs emit green luminescence (515nm) when these materials were excited at a 460nm UV beam, with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 367 and 444nm, respectively (Fig. 2c). In addition, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs-PEGs are at 500 and 512nm, respectively (Fig. 2d). The PL spectra of GQDs-PEIs and GQDs-PEGs show varying degrees of the red shift phenomenon compared with GQDs; this finding implies grain growth. These results are consistent with the quantum confinement effect [25].

Fig. 3 further confirms the quality of the as-prepared GQDs. The Raman spectra (Fig. 3a) shows that the disordered (D) band at 1356cm−1 is associated with the presence of sp
3 defects; the crystalline (G) band at 1578cm−1 is related to the in-plane vibration of sp
2 carbon [26]. The ratio of the intensities (ID/IG) of these characteristic bands can be used to correlate the structural properties of the carbon. ID/IG of GQDs is 0.97, which is an indication for electron transfer. GQDs have rich oxygen-containing functional groups (OCFGs), as indicated in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3b). The typical band at 3441cm−1 is assigned to the –OH group linked to the composites. The broad absorption bands at 3000–3500cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. These results indicate that numerous amino and hydroxyl groups are present on the surface of GQDs; as such, the GQDs exhibit good hydrophilic properties. Moreover, GQDs-PEGs yield a characteristic peak at 2885cm−1 because of the CH bonding vibration. The bands at 1709 and 1667cm−1 are attributed to the vibrational absorption bands of CO in COOH and NH in CONH, respectively. The transmittance of OH decreased in GQDs-PEIs and GQDs-PEGs. Compared to PEI, the transmittance of NH decreased in GQDs-PEIs and the transmittance of CH increased in GQDs-PEIs. Also, compared to PEG, CC absorption band occurred in GQDs-PEGs. This finding indicates that GQDs are modified by PEI and PEG through a dehydration condensation reaction.

GQDs contain large amounts of OCFGs because of citric acid as a precursor, and this finding is also indicated in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3b). OCFGs provide the GQDs with great polarity; as such, the GQDs could dissolve in water and produce more free radicals conveniently. More ·OH species were generated, and their concentration increased gradually with irradiation time and synergy of GQDs [31]. The Raman spectra (Fig. 3a) also suggest that GQDs are prone to transfer electrons to functionalising groups, which allowed the photoelectrons that occurred under visible light to spread easily and promoted oxygen and water to produce free radicals that degraded MB·PEI and PEG wrapped GQDs to reduce the generation of free radicals. However, PEI provides some comparative advantages over PEG. PEI was widely investigated because of high concentrations of positively charged nitrogen atoms that facilitate effective binding and high contents of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines on PEI [18]. By contrast, PEG contains one hydroxyl group on one side. This characteristic shows that GQDs-PEIs comprise more GQD particles than GQDs-PEGs because the former contains numerous amino groups. Therefore, the degradation rate of MB by GQDs-PEGs was the lowest. As a result, the degradation rate could be qualitatively controlled by using different polymer-modified GQDs.

In summary, GQDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method by using citric acid as a precursor. GQDs were modified with PEI and PEG. The modified GQDs exhibited excitation-independent photoluminescence under UV light because of their highly uniform sizes and bright blue photoluminescence. The highly fluorescent GQDs were highly soluble in water and stable. The photocatalytic performance results suggest that GQDs could function as a visible light photocatalyst in the degradation of organic matter. Moreover, the degradation rate of organic matter could be qualitatively controlled by different polymer-modified GQDs in visible light. The photocatalytic rate of GQDs, GQDs-PEIs, and GQDs-PEGs decreased successively. Thus, photocatalytic organic matter degradation, sustained drug release, and tracking can be combined to implement proper sewage disposal.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51572184, 51372160, and 51172152).
